content.full_text:
May 20, 1977

A-77-443

Mark Schneider
San Diego Socialist Workers Party
1053 - 15th Street
San Diego, CA 92101

Dear Mr. Schneider:

We have received your letter of May 11, 1977, in which you ask if the Commission would consider exempting the San Diego Socialist Workers Party from certain requirements of the San Diego Municipal Election Ordinance.

The Commission has no jurisdiction to consider requests for exemptions from the requirements of any municipal ordinance. Our jurisdiction extends only to disclosure obligations imposed by the State Political Reform Act. Thus, the Commission has no authority to entertain your request.

With respect to the State Act, however, you should be aware that you are not presently exempt from any of its campaign disclosure requirements. Candidates from the Socialist Workers Party in Oakland and Los Angeles received temporary exemptions from the Commission in March of this year, but those exemptions apply only to the April municipal elections in which they were candidates. An application has been filed by the Los Angeles branch of the Socialist Workers Party for a statewide exemption for all SWP candidates for the next two years, but that application has not yet been heard. Accordingly, if you desire to seek an exemption from the disclosure requirements of the State Act for the candidates intend to run in the forthcoming San Diego election, you should submit an application to us in accordance with the procedures outlined in the enclosed regulation. Please note particularly that the regulation requires that a request for an exemption be submitted 30 days prior to any filing deadline, unless good cause is shown for filing the application late.

If I can answer any further questions concerning this procedure, please contact me.

Sincerely,


Michael J. Baker
Chief
Legal Division

MJB:plh
Enclosure

San Diego Socialist Workers Party
1053 15th St.
San Diego, Ca.,92101
May 11,1977


Fair Political Practices Commission

1100 K. St.
Sacramento, Ca


To the Commissioners :

The San Diego Socialist Workers Party will announce two candidates for City
Council seats #1 and #3 and a candidate for school board seat C in the 1977
elections. Although the elections are non-partisan, the candidates will be
publically identified as SWP members.

Petitions for nomination are available June 22 and returned July1. July
22 is the last date petitions may be returned. Statements of financial intent
must be submitted at the time of filing and financial reports are due on August
18th and Sept.13th offr the primary. Candidates surviving the primary file
additional reports Nov. 1 and Jan. 12,1978.

As you know, the SWP has sought exemption from disclosing the names of its
contributors at the national, state and local levels. We seek exemption from
disclosure under San Diego's Municipal Election Code Ordinance #11034 Section
27.2932(Campaign Statements -contents)and 27.2912 (Campaign Treasurer-Duties).
It seems to me we should also be exempt from 27.2921,27.2922 and 27.2926
which have to do with the establishment of a campaign trust fund. It seems
to me these last regulations also require disclosure.

We would like the FPPC to consider exempting us from these regulations
for the following reasons:

1)Precedents set by exemptions granted SWP candidates in 1977 in Oakland
and Los Angeles.

2) San Diego supporters of SWP candidates have often been subject to
harassment. The 1974 SWP suit seeking exemption from the California disclosure
law includes depositions by San Diegans Marc Rich, Bonnie Cady, Jo Della-
Giustina, Page Tulloch,Vincent Longo and others regarding instances of
harassment.

Rich and Cady both were fired from their jobs subsequent to illegal
visits to employers by FBI agents. Della Giustina almost lost an apartment,
Tulloch's relations with his parents were severely strained,etc; all as a
result of FBI harassment.

3) The election laws are applied in an unjust manner against our candidates. In 1973 SWP city council candidate Salm Kolis was falsely charged, in our view, with an election infraction. At the end of the 1973 election, the City Attorney reported that 22 of 29 candidates had violated some aspect of the election code. Yet only Kolis was prosecuted. Kolis was convicted, spent time in jail, paid a $500 fine and was placed on probation for 3 years for allegedly listing an incorrect address.

4) The San Diego District Attorney's January 27,1977 report to the City Council questions the validity of 27.2912 and other aspects of the code.

Thank you for your prompt attention.

Sincerely,

[illegible]

Mark Schneider
SWP San Diego Chairperson
